pattern: The number of children satisfiable at a given pile size shrinks monotonically as the size grows, so binary searching over the candy count per child -- summing floor(pile / size) across piles -- finds the largest feasible size directly instead of simulating any split.
hints: If you fix a target number of candies per child, you can check in one linear pass whether it's achievable: sum up how many whole piles of that size each candy pile yields.

As the target per-child amount increases, the number of children you can satisfy can only decrease or stay the same -- that monotonic relationship means you can binary search on the answer instead of trying every size.

Binary search the per-child amount between 1 and the largest pile. For each candidate, sum floor(candies[i] / candidate) over all piles; if that sum is >= k the candidate is feasible, otherwise it's too big. Watch the case where even one candy each isn't possible.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, some piles must be split
statement example, more children than total candies
single pile, single child, gets the whole pile
k so large no split can give even one candy each
all piles identical, answer equals the pile size itself
k equals total candy count exactly, answer must be 1
single tiny pile smaller than k, nobody can get any candy
one large pile among many tiny ones, only the large pile is worth splitting

stress.description: random pile sizes with k spanning small and huge magnitudes
